title: Linear Inequalities
blurb: Solving and graphing inequalities, the negative-flip rule, compound 'and/or' conditions, and absolute-value equations — algebra for ranges instead of single answers.
buildsOn: Solving linear equations (Unit 2) and the number line.

lessons.title: Solving & Graphing Inequalities
lessons.concreteComparison: An equation pins down one number; an inequality fences off a whole region. 'You must be at least 140 cm to ride' doesn't name YOUR height — it describes every height that gets on the ride: $h \ge 140$.
lessons.objective: Solve one- and two-step inequalities, apply the flip rule when multiplying or dividing by a negative, and graph solutions on a number line.
concept: An **inequality** compares with $<, \le, >, \ge$ and its solution is a SET of numbers, drawn on a number line: an **open circle** for $<$ or $>$ (endpoint excluded), a **closed circle** for $\le$ or $\ge$ (endpoint included), and a ray showing the direction.

Adding or subtracting on both sides works exactly as for equations. So does multiplying or dividing by a POSITIVE number. But **multiplying or dividing both sides by a NEGATIVE number reverses the inequality**: from $3 < 5$, multiplying by $-1$ gives $-3 > -5$. The order of the number line flips with the signs.
Because solutions are infinite sets, the check works by sampling: pick one number from your answer region and one from outside, and test both in the original inequality.
lessons.keyIdea: Solve inequalities like equations, but FLIP the symbol whenever you multiply or divide both sides by a negative — then graph with open/closed circle and a ray.

facts.title: The flip rule
facts.explanation: Dividing by $-2$ reverses the symbol. Only ×/÷ by a negative flips — never adding or subtracting.

facts.title: Circles on the graph
facts.explanation: Closed circle = endpoint counts ($\le, \ge$); open circle = it doesn't ($<, >$).

workedExamples.solution: Add 7: $4x \le 16$. Divide by 4 (positive — no flip): $x \le 4$. Graph: closed circle at 4, ray to the left. Sample check: $x = 0$ gives $-7 \le 9$ ✓; $x = 5$ gives $13 \le 9$ ✗.
Subtract 5: $-2x > 6$. Divide by $-2$ and FLIP: $x < -3$. Sample check: $x = -4$ gives $5 + 8 = 13 > 11$ ✓; $x = 0$ gives $5 > 11$ ✗.

commonMistakes.correction: Dividing by $-2$ reverses the symbol: $x < -3$. Test a number — $x = 0$ fails the original, so the region must lie left of $-3$.

commonMistakes.text: Flipping when merely subtracting a negative-looking term.
commonMistakes.correction: Only MULTIPLYING or DIVIDING by a negative flips. Moving terms with $+$ or $-$ never does.

tryIt.solution: $3x > -6$, so $x > -2$. Open circle at $-2$, ray right. Check $x = 0$: $5 > -1$ ✓.

steps.title: Rules are ranges
steps.body: 'At least 140 cm to ride.' 'Under \$50.' 'No more than 8 attempts.' Real-world rules rarely name one number — they fence off a RANGE. Inequalities are the algebra of ranges: same solving moves as equations, one extra twist, and answers you draw instead of circle.

steps.title: Feel the flip
steps.teach: Slide the marker and watch its opposite. $3 < 5$, but their opposites satisfy $-3 > -5$: negating REFLECTS the number line through zero, so 'greater' and 'less' trade places. That reflection is why multiplying an inequality by a negative must flip the symbol.

steps.title: One twist only
steps.body: Add or subtract on both sides: nothing changes. Multiply or divide by a POSITIVE: nothing changes. Multiply or divide by a NEGATIVE: **the symbol reverses.** From $-2x > 6$, dividing by $-2$ gives $x < -3$. That's the entire difference between inequalities and equations.

steps.title: Divide by a negative
steps.explanation: Divide by $-3$, flip: $x \ge -4$. Test $x = 0$: $0 \le 12$ ✓, and 0 is in the region $x \ge -4$. ✓

steps.title: Circle, then ray
steps.body: Draw the endpoint first: **closed circle** if the symbol includes equality ($\le, \ge$), **open circle** if not ($<, >$). Then shade the ray in the direction of the solutions. $x \le 4$: closed at 4, shade left. $x > -2$: open at $-2$, shade right.

steps.title: Read a graph back
steps.prompt: A graph shows an OPEN circle at $5$ with shading to the right. Which inequality is it?
steps.explanation: Open circle → 5 itself excluded → strict. Shading right → greater. $x > 5$.

steps.title: Sample one in, one out
steps.body: After solving, test TWO numbers in the original inequality: one from inside your answer region (should work) and one from outside (should fail). This catches a missed flip instantly — a flipped-wrong answer fails both tests.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Inequalities solve like equations — except ×/÷ by a negative FLIPS the symbol.
Closed circle for $\le, \ge$; open for $<, >$; ray shows the region.
Check by sampling one number inside the region and one outside.

lessons.title: Multi-Step Inequalities & Applications
lessons.concreteComparison: Budgeting is inequality solving: with \$110 at the fair, a \$20 entry fee, and \$15 rides, 'how many rides CAN I afford' is not an equation — it's $15r + 20 \le 110$, and the answer is every $r$ up to 6.
lessons.objective: Solve inequalities with variables on both sides and parentheses, and translate 'at least / at most' situations into inequalities.
concept: Multi-step inequalities follow the equation pipeline — distribute, combine, collect variables, collect constants — with the flip rule on duty whenever a negative multiplies or divides both sides.
Phrase dictionary: **at least** → $\ge$; **at most / no more than** → $\le$; **more than / exceeds** → $>$; **less than / under** → $<$; **minimum** pairs with $\ge$, **maximum** with $\le$.

Real answers respect context: if rides cost \$15 with a \$20 fee and you have \$110, then $15r + 20 \le 110$ gives $r \le 6$ — and since $r$ counts rides, the realistic answers are $0, 1, \ldots, 6$. Algebra gives the fence; context picks which numbers inside it make sense.
lessons.keyIdea: Run the equation pipeline with the flip rule armed, translate 'at least/at most' to ≥/≤, and let the context trim the solution set.

facts.title: Same pipeline
facts.explanation: Identical to equations — plus one guard: dividing by a negative flips.

workedExamples.statement: A tutoring service charges a \$30 registration fee plus \$25 per session. Bilguun's budget is at most \$180. How many sessions can he take?
workedExamples.solution: $25s + 30 \le 180 \to 25s \le 150 \to s \le 6$. He can take at most 6 sessions (6 costs exactly $150 + 30 = 180$).

commonMistakes.correction: 'At least 8' includes 8: $x \ge 8$. Strict vs. inclusive is exactly the difference between $>$ and $\ge$.

commonMistakes.text: Reporting $s \le 6.4$ sessions as the final answer.
commonMistakes.correction: Sessions are whole: the practical answer is 'at most 6 sessions.' Round DOWN when the variable counts things and the fence is an upper bound.

tryIt.solution: Subtract $8x$: $-3x - 2 \ge 13$. Add 2: $-3x \ge 15$. Divide by $-3$ and FLIP: $x \le -5$. Check $x = -6$: $-32 \ge -35$ ✓.

tryIt.statement: A lift holds at most 600 kg. A worker (80 kg) rides with boxes of 32 kg each. How many boxes can ride along?

steps.title: 'Can I afford it?' is an inequality
steps.body: Almost every budget question — money, weight limits, time limits, passing scores — asks for a RANGE of workable values, not a single one. The algebra is Unit 2's pipeline; the new skills are translating the phrases and reading the answer sensibly.

steps.title: At least vs. more than
steps.body: **At least 140** means 140 is allowed: $h \ge 140$. **More than 140** means it isn't: $h > 140$. Same on the other side: **at most** → $\le$, **less than** → $<$. One word — 'at' — decides whether the boundary itself belongs.

steps.title: Boundary in or out?
steps.prompt: 'You need at least 75 points to pass.' Which inequality describes passing scores $p$?

steps.title: Context trims the set
steps.body: $b \le 16.25$ boxes — but a quarter-box doesn't ride the lift. When the variable counts whole things, the algebraic fence gets trimmed by reality: at most 16 boxes. For MINIMUM questions the trim goes the other way: 'at least 6.2 buses' becomes 7 buses. **Round toward safety.**

steps.title: Round the right way
steps.prompt: A club needs at least \$500 from a bake sale at \$3 per item. Solving gives $n \ge 166.7$. How many items must they sell?
steps.explanation: 166 items brings only \$498 — short. The first WHOLE number satisfying the fence is 167. Minimum requirements round UP.

steps.title: Answer the question, not the algebra
steps.body: Finish word problems with a sentence: 'He can take at most 6 sessions.' The sentence forces you to reconcile the algebraic fence with the real-world units — the step where rounding direction and boundary inclusion get checked for free.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Same solving pipeline as equations, flip rule armed.
When variables count things: round DOWN for maxima, UP for minima.

lessons.title: Compound Inequalities
lessons.concreteComparison: A thermostat set 'between 19° and 23°' enforces TWO conditions at once — above 19 AND below 23. A storm warning 'below −20° or above 35°' is satisfied by EITHER extreme. 'And' intersects; 'or' unions.
lessons.objective: Solve 'and' (between) and 'or' compound inequalities and graph their solution sets.
concept: An **'and' compound** demands both conditions: $-3 < x \le 4$ means $x > -3$ AND $x \le 4$ — the OVERLAP, a segment. Solve the three-part form by doing each move to ALL THREE parts: from $-1 \le 2x + 3 < 9$, subtract 3 everywhere ($-4 \le 2x < 6$), divide by 2 ($-2 \le x < 3$).
An **'or' compound** accepts either condition: $x < -2$ or $x > 5$ — two rays pointing away from each other, a union. Solve each piece separately and keep both.

Direction check: 'and' regions are usually BOUNDED (between two fences); 'or' regions usually point OUTWARD. If your 'and' answer comes out empty or your 'or' covers everything, re-read — it can happen, but it should make sense.
lessons.keyIdea: 'And' = intersection (a segment; operate on all three parts at once); 'or' = union (two outward rays; solve each piece separately).

facts.title: Three-part moves
facts.explanation: Whatever you do, do to LEFT, MIDDLE, and RIGHT.

facts.title: And vs. or
facts.explanation: 'And' keeps only numbers passing BOTH tests; 'or' keeps numbers passing at least one.

workedExamples.solution: Subtract 1 from all parts: $-6 \le 3x < 9$. Divide all by 3: $-2 \le x < 3$. Graph: closed at $-2$, open at 3, segment between. Check $x = 0$: $1$ is between $-5$ and $10$ ✓.
Left: $2x < -6$, $x < -3$. Right: $3x \ge 12$, $x \ge 4$. Solution: $x < -3$ or $x \ge 4$ — two rays. Check $x = 0$: fails both ✓ (0 is in the gap).

commonMistakes.text: Operating on only two of the three parts of $-1 \le 2x + 3 < 9$.
commonMistakes.correction: The compound is one statement with three columns — every move hits all three.

commonMistakes.text: Writing an 'or' answer as a single between-statement: '$x < -3$ or $x \ge 4$' as $4 \le x < -3$.
commonMistakes.correction: Outward rays can't be written as one 'between'. Keep the word 'or' — it IS the answer's shape.

tryIt.solution: Multiply all parts by 2: $2 < x - 3 \le 8$. Add 3: $5 < x \le 11$. Check $x = 7$: $\frac{4}{2} = 2$, and $1 < 2 \le 4$ ✓.

steps.title: Two fences at once
steps.body: A greenhouse must stay between 19° and 23°. One inequality can't say that — it takes two conditions joined by AND: $t > 19$ and $t < 23$, compressed into $19 < t < 23$. Other rules trigger on EITHER extreme — 'alert if below −20° OR above 35°.' Two joiners, two very different pictures.

steps.title: The between sandwich
steps.body: $-1 \le 2x + 3 < 9$ is three columns sharing one $x$. Operate on ALL THREE at once: subtract 3 everywhere → $-4 \le 2x < 6$; divide by 2 → $-2 \le x < 3$. The answer is the overlap: a SEGMENT with one closed end and one open end.

steps.title: All three columns
steps.explanation: Subtract 5 from all three parts: $-2 \le x < 3$. The symbols keep their styles — closed left, open right.

steps.title: Either fence works
steps.body: $2x + 1 < -5$ **or** $3x - 4 \ge 8$: solve each piece on its own ($x < -3$; $x \ge 4$) and keep BOTH — the union. The graph is two rays pointing away from each other with a gap between. A number qualifies by passing EITHER test; only the gap fails both.

steps.title: Who's in the set?
steps.prompt: The solution set is $x < -1$ or $x \ge 6$. Which number is IN it?
steps.explanation: $-2 < -1$ passes the first test. The others sit in the gap $[-1, 6)$ and fail both.

steps.title: Draw before deciding
steps.body: Sketch both conditions on one number line BEFORE writing the answer. 'And' answers are where the shadings overlap; 'or' answers are everything shaded at all. The picture makes union-vs-intersection mistakes impossible.

steps.title: What sticks
points: 'And' = overlap = segment; operate on all three parts together.
'Or' = union = outward rays; solve each piece separately.
Sketch both conditions on one line — the picture decides.

lessons.title: Absolute-Value Equations & Inequalities
lessons.concreteComparison: A bottling machine targets 500 ml with a tolerance of 5 ml. 'Within 5 of 500' is a DISTANCE statement: $|v - 500| \le 5$. Absolute value is how algebra talks about distance from a target.
lessons.objective: Solve absolute-value equations and inequalities by translating them into distance statements, and recognize the no-solution cases.
concept: $|x|$ is the **distance from $x$ to 0** — never negative. $|x| = 5$ has TWO solutions, $x = 5$ and $x = -5$: two points at distance 5. More generally $|X| = k$ (with $k > 0$) splits into $X = k$ or $X = -k$.

Inequalities read as distances too. $|X| < k$ means 'within $k$ of zero': the SEGMENT $-k < X < k$ (an 'and'). $|X| > k$ means 'farther than $k$': the two rays $X < -k$ or $X > k$ (an 'or'). Memory hook: less-th**an** → 'and'; great-**or** → 'or'.
**Isolate the absolute value first**, and watch for impossible demands: $|X| = -3$ or $|X| < -1$ have NO solution (distance can't be negative), while $|X| \ge -2$ is true for ALL numbers.
lessons.keyIdea: Read |X| as distance: |X| = k splits into ±k; |X| < k is the segment between; |X| > k is the rays beyond — and a negative right side means no solutions (or all).

facts.title: Equation splits in two
facts.explanation: Two points sit at distance $k$ from zero.

facts.title: Greater-than is beyond
facts.explanation: 'Farther than $k$' is two rays — an OR.

workedExamples.statement: A machine fills bottles to $v$ ml with target 500 and tolerance 5: $|v - 500| \le 5$. What volumes pass?
workedExamples.solution: Between form: $-5 \le v - 500 \le 5$, so $495 \le v \le 505$. Any fill from 495 to 505 ml passes.

commonMistakes.text: Solving $|2x - 3| = 7$ with only the positive branch.
commonMistakes.correction: Distance 7 happens on BOTH sides: split into $2x - 3 = 7$ AND $2x - 3 = -7$. Two answers.
That's the LESS-than picture. Greater-than means farther away: $X < -k$ or $X > k$.

steps.title: Within 5 of the target
steps.body: Quality control never demands EXACTLY 500 ml — it demands 'within 5 ml of 500.' That's a statement about DISTANCE, and $|v - 500| \le 5$ is its algebra. Master the distance reading and every absolute-value problem becomes a picture.

steps.title: Distance from zero
steps.teach: Slide the marker: $|x|$ is how FAR it sits from zero, ignoring direction. Notice $|4| = |-4| = 4$ — two different positions, one distance. That's why $|x| = 4$ has two solutions.

steps.title: Split into two
steps.body: $|2x - 3| = 7$ says: the quantity $2x - 3$ sits at distance 7 from zero — so it's either $7$ or $-7$. Solve both little equations, get both answers: $x = 5$ or $x = -2$. **One absolute value, two branches, always.**

steps.title: How many solutions?
steps.explanation: Distance ZERO from 6 means standing exactly ON 6: the single solution $x = 6$. (And $|X| = -2$ would have none — distance can't be negative.)

steps.title: Between vs. beyond
steps.body: $|X| < 5$: within 5 of zero → the segment $-5 < X < 5$ (an AND). $|X| > 5$: farther than 5 → the rays $X < -5$ or $X > 5$ (an OR). Hook: less-th**AN**d, great-**OR**. Draw the two pictures once and you'll never mix them.

steps.title: Pick the picture
steps.explanation: Farther than 3 from the number 2: beyond $2 - 3 = -1$ on the left OR beyond $2 + 3 = 5$ on the right.

steps.title: Isolate the bars first
steps.body: In $2|x - 1| + 3 = 11$, get the absolute value ALONE before splitting: $|x - 1| = 4$, then $x = 5$ or $x = -3$. Splitting while other terms still cling to the bars produces two wrong equations.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $|X| < k$ → between ($-k < X < k$); $|X| > k$ → beyond (two rays).
Isolate the absolute value first; negative right side → no solutions.

practice.statement: Concert tickets cost \$18 each plus a \$6 booking fee per order. With at most \$100, how many tickets can one order contain?

testYourself.statement: A parcel service accepts boxes whose weight $w$ satisfies $|w - 12| \le 2.5$ kg. What weights are accepted?
